nodes.label: Foundations of Reasoning Beyond the Text
nodes.summary: Reasoning Beyond the Text questions ask the test-taker to use the passage's ideas in situations the author did not explicitly address, by either applying them to new contexts or combining them with new information.

nodes.label: Definition of the RB skill
nodes.summary: RB is the third and most demanding CARS skill, requiring the reader to take the passage beyond its own boundaries while staying faithful to its logic.

nodes.label: Reasoning beyond the text, defined
nodes.summary: RB requires extending or testing passage ideas against material that lies outside the passage itself, rather than merely locating or restating what was stated.

nodes.label: The two RB sub-skills
nodes.summary: AAMC divides RB into Applying/Extrapolating the passage's ideas to new contexts and Incorporating new information that may support, challenge, or modify the passage.

nodes.label: Proportion of RB on the exam
nodes.summary: Reasoning Beyond the Text accounts for roughly 40% of CARS questions, the largest of the three skill categories.

nodes.label: RB versus Foundations of Comprehension
nodes.summary: Unlike FOC, which is answered from information stated or directly implied in the passage, RB introduces material or scenarios the passage never mentions.

nodes.label: RB versus Reasoning Within the Text
nodes.summary: RW evaluates and integrates ideas internal to the passage, whereas RB brings the passage into contact with external contexts or new facts.

nodes.label: How the three CARS skills relate
nodes.summary: FOC, RW, and RB form a ladder: comprehend the text, reason within it, then reason beyond it; RB presupposes the first two and is the only skill that imports material from outside the passage.

nodes.label: Grasping the passage before reasoning beyond it
nodes.summary: Every RB move depends on first having a secure grip on the author's main idea, purpose, tone, and argument.

nodes.label: Main idea as the anchor for extension
nodes.summary: A correct RB answer must remain consistent with the passage's central thesis, so the main idea is the test against which any extension is checked.

nodes.label: Author's purpose constrains the extension
nodes.summary: What the author is trying to accomplish (persuade, critique, describe, reconcile) bounds which new applications and new information are relevant.

nodes.label: Isolating the argument's core claim and support
nodes.summary: Knowing the precise claim and the evidence offered for it tells you what a new application would have to preserve or what new information would strengthen or weaken.

nodes.label: Extracting the transferable principle
nodes.summary: RB hinges on distilling the abstract, generalizable principle behind a passage's specific example so it can be carried to a different domain.

nodes.label: Awareness of the passage's scope and limits
nodes.summary: Recognizing how far the author's claim is meant to reach prevents over-extending an idea into territory the author would not endorse.

nodes.label: Reading tone and attitude as constraints
nodes.summary: The author's tone (skeptical, admiring, ironic, cautious) signals which extensions and judgments are faithful and which clash with how the author feels about the subject.

nodes.label: Identifying the author's underlying values
nodes.summary: Many RB predictions and judgments turn on the value commitments implicit in the passage, which become the basis for projecting the author into new situations.

nodes.label: The fidelity principle
nodes.summary: However far an RB question travels from the passage, the credited answer must remain loyal to the author's reasoning, values, and conclusions.

nodes.label: Staying faithful to authorial logic
nodes.summary: The right answer is the one the author would most plausibly accept given the reasoning actually presented, not the one the test-taker personally prefers.

nodes.label: No outside subject-matter knowledge required
nodes.summary: CARS answers are derivable from the passage plus the stimulus given; prior expertise in the topic is never needed and can mislead.

nodes.label: Tracking the author's voice and attitude
nodes.summary: Holding the author's tone and value commitments in mind keeps an extension or new-information judgment aligned with how the author actually thinks.

nodes.label: Honoring the spirit, not just the letter
nodes.summary: A faithful extension captures what the author meant and would endorse, even where the exact words don't appear, while resisting choices that quote the passage but betray its intent.

nodes.label: Evaluating RB answer choices
nodes.summary: RB answer choices are vetted against the passage's logic, scope, and direction rather than against memorized content.

nodes.label: Watching degree and qualifier language
nodes.summary: Absolute words (always, never, must) usually overreach, while measured qualifiers (may, some, often) better match the cautious scope of most passages.

nodes.label: Checking the direction of the answer
nodes.summary: A choice that points opposite to the author's stance or to the question's demand (e.g., weakens when asked to strengthen) is wrong even if it is true.

nodes.label: Filtering for relevance to the question task
nodes.summary: A true or appealing statement is still wrong if it does not address the specific RB task the question stem sets.

nodes.label: Common RB distractor patterns
nodes.summary: RB traps recur as out-of-scope, opposite-direction, half-right/half-wrong, too-extreme, and true-but-irrelevant choices.

nodes.label: The out-of-scope distractor
nodes.summary: The most common RB trap introduces a consideration the passage never raises and the stimulus does not license, making the choice irrelevant however plausible.

nodes.label: The half-right / half-wrong distractor
nodes.summary: A choice can open with an accurate restatement of the passage and then attach a clause that distorts, overstates, or reverses it, so the whole choice must be read.

nodes.label: The too-extreme distractor
nodes.summary: A choice can match the passage's direction but push it to an absolute or radical degree the author never claimed, failing the degree test.

nodes.label: The true-but-irrelevant distractor
nodes.summary: A statement can be factually or even passage-consistently true yet fail to perform the specific RB task the stem demands, so truth alone never earns the credit.

nodes.label: The distortion / subtle-twist distractor
nodes.summary: A choice that swaps a key term, shifts the relationship, or changes the agent of an action mirrors the passage closely while reversing or warping its meaning.

nodes.label: Applying and Extrapolating Ideas to New Contexts
nodes.summary: This RB sub-skill takes the passage's ideas and uses them to interpret, predict, or judge situations the passage never discussed.

nodes.label: Transferring a principle to a new domain
nodes.summary: The core move is recognizing the abstract principle in the passage and mapping it onto an unrelated concrete situation.

nodes.label: Mapping an abstract principle to a concrete case
nodes.summary: Application questions ask which concrete scenario best instantiates the general principle the author articulated.

nodes.label: Recognizing structural rather than surface similarity
nodes.summary: A correct application matches the underlying logical structure of the passage idea, not merely the surface topic or vocabulary.

nodes.label: Cross-domain mapping
nodes.summary: Application often moves a humanities idea into a wholly different field (science, business, everyday life), keeping only the relationship intact.

nodes.label: Surface-similarity trap
nodes.summary: A wrong choice may reuse the passage's topic words while violating its actual principle, exploiting superficial matching.

nodes.label: Applying an idea to a practical or policy case
nodes.summary: Some application questions ask which real-world practice, decision, or policy the author's principle would endorse or condemn, testing transfer from theory to action.

nodes.label: Selecting the best example of an idea
nodes.summary: A frequent RB task gives several scenarios and asks which one the author's idea best fits, or which least fits.

nodes.label: Identifying the best illustrative example
nodes.summary: The credited example fully satisfies every defining feature of the passage concept, not just some of them.

nodes.label: EXCEPT / LEAST-fit example questions
nodes.summary: When asked which scenario the principle does NOT fit, the answer is the single choice that fails the defining criteria the others meet.

nodes.label: Building a criteria checklist for the concept
nodes.summary: Listing the necessary features of the passage's concept lets you test each candidate scenario systematically.

nodes.label: Discriminating partial from full fit
nodes.summary: Several scenarios may satisfy some criteria; the credited one satisfies all, so the task is rejecting choices that fit only partially.

nodes.label: Predicting author response or outcome
nodes.summary: Some RB questions ask what the author would say, do, or expect in a hypothetical the passage did not cover.

nodes.label: Predicting how the author would respond
nodes.summary: The answer is inferred from the author's demonstrated values and reasoning, projecting them onto the new situation.

nodes.label: Predicting the likely consequence of a view
nodes.summary: Extrapolation can require forecasting the plausible result if the author's principle were carried out or accepted.

nodes.label: Reasoning through hypotheticals
nodes.summary: RB hypotheticals ("suppose that...") must be answered by running the passage's logic forward into the imagined case.

nodes.label: Judging what the author would agree or disagree with
nodes.summary: The credited statement is the one most consistent with the author's stance; the wrong ones clash with or are silent to that stance.

nodes.label: Predicting the author's reaction to a rival view
nodes.summary: A common stem presents an outside thinker's claim and asks how the author would react, requiring projection of the author's stance onto an opposing position.

nodes.label: Projecting the author's value judgment
nodes.summary: Where the passage reveals what the author prizes or condemns, an RB question may ask the author to evaluate a new case as good/bad, justified/unjustified.

nodes.label: Generalizing from passage specifics
nodes.summary: Extrapolation can mean drawing the broader rule that the passage's particular instances point toward.

nodes.label: Moving from specific instances to a general rule
nodes.summary: A valid generalization captures exactly the pattern the passage's examples share without claiming more than they support.

nodes.label: Avoiding overgeneralization
nodes.summary: A trap answer stretches a limited passage point into a sweeping universal claim the evidence cannot bear.

nodes.label: Respecting the boundary conditions of a claim
nodes.summary: A sound extrapolation honors the conditions under which the author's claim holds and does not apply it where those conditions fail.

nodes.label: Scope-shift trap (broadening or narrowing)
nodes.summary: A distractor quietly widens or narrows the population, domain, or claim of the passage, producing a statement the author's evidence does not actually license.

nodes.label: Analogical reasoning
nodes.summary: RB frequently tests the ability to recognize, complete, or evaluate analogies built on the passage's relationships.

nodes.label: Matching the relationship in an analogy
nodes.summary: A correct analogy preserves the relationship between elements, not the identity of the elements themselves.

nodes.label: Choosing the most analogous situation
nodes.summary: "Most analogous" questions reward the choice whose internal relationship mirrors the passage's relationship most exactly.

nodes.label: Detecting a flawed or weak analogy
nodes.summary: An analogy fails when the cases differ in a respect that is relevant to the point being made.

nodes.label: Evaluating the author's own analogy
nodes.summary: When the passage itself uses an analogy, RB can ask which new case it best fits or where the analogy breaks down, testing comprehension of its limits.

nodes.label: Synthesizing across passage ideas
nodes.summary: Some RB items require combining two or more of the passage's ideas, or the passage with the stimulus, to reach a conclusion neither states alone.

nodes.label: Combining two passage ideas into a new conclusion
nodes.summary: The credited answer follows only when two separately stated passage points are joined, a move the surface-level distractors miss.

nodes.label: Integrating the passage with new stimulus material
nodes.summary: Many RB stems pair the passage with a fresh fact or scenario; the answer must respect both the passage logic and the added material together.

nodes.label: Incorporating Information into the Passage
nodes.summary: This RB sub-skill introduces new facts, claims, or scenarios and asks how they interact with the passage's argument.

nodes.label: Assessing the effect of new information
nodes.summary: The central task is to judge whether new information strengthens, weakens, or leaves unchanged the author's claim.

nodes.label: Information that strengthens the argument
nodes.summary: New evidence strengthens when it supplies additional support for, or removes an objection to, the author's central claim.

nodes.label: Information that weakens the argument
nodes.summary: New evidence weakens when it contradicts a key premise, undermines the link between premise and conclusion, or offers a competing explanation.

nodes.label: Information that has no effect
nodes.summary: Information is irrelevant when it neither supports nor challenges the specific claim, often because it is out of scope.

nodes.label: "Most" strengthen / weaken questions
nodes.summary: When several choices affect the argument, the credited answer is the one with the largest impact on the central claim.

nodes.label: Weighing degree of impact
nodes.summary: Evaluating new information requires gauging how much, not merely whether, it moves the strength of the argument.

nodes.label: Weaken versus disprove (degree confusion)
nodes.summary: A strengthen/weaken task asks for a shift in the argument's plausibility, not proof or refutation; choices that claim to definitively prove or disprove usually overreach.

nodes.label: Targeting the right part of the argument
nodes.summary: New information acts on a specific premise, assumption, or conclusion, so locating that target is essential.

nodes.label: Identifying the conclusion under attack or support
nodes.summary: Pinpointing the exact conclusion the question concerns prevents crediting information that affects a different claim.

nodes.label: Acting on an unstated assumption
nodes.summary: New information often strengthens or weakens by confirming or denying an assumption that bridges the author's premise to conclusion.

nodes.label: Introducing an alternative explanation
nodes.summary: A common weakener offers a rival cause or interpretation that accounts for the same evidence the author used.

nodes.label: Using a counterexample
nodes.summary: A single well-chosen counterexample can weaken a universal claim by showing a case the claim cannot accommodate.

nodes.label: Attacking or supporting a stated premise
nodes.summary: New information can act directly on a premise the author asserts, raising or lowering its credibility and thereby the conclusion's support.

nodes.label: Judging consistency with the passage
nodes.summary: Some questions ask whether a new statement is consistent with, contradicts, or is independent of the author's position.

nodes.label: New claim consistent with the passage
nodes.summary: A statement is consistent when it can be true at the same time as the author's claims without conflict.

nodes.label: New claim that contradicts the passage
nodes.summary: A statement contradicts the passage when it cannot be true together with a claim the author endorses.

nodes.label: Reconciling apparent conflicts
nodes.summary: Some questions test whether a new fact only seems to conflict but can be reconciled with the passage on closer reading.

nodes.label: Recognizing a logically independent claim
nodes.summary: A new statement may be neither supported nor contradicted by the passage because the two address different matters, making it logically independent.

nodes.label: Modifying or revising the author's view
nodes.summary: New information can force a question about how the author's position would have to change in response.

nodes.label: How new evidence would modify the thesis
nodes.summary: The answer states the minimal, logically required adjustment to the author's claim given the new fact.

nodes.label: Whether the thesis survives the new information
nodes.summary: RB can ask whether new information merely qualifies the thesis or fatally undermines it, requiring a judgment of degree.

nodes.label: Preferring the minimal required change
nodes.summary: When asked how a view must change, the credited answer makes the smallest revision the new fact compels, not the most dramatic overhaul among the choices.

nodes.label: Incorporating data, studies, and findings
nodes.summary: Some RB stimuli present hypothetical research results and ask how they bear on a humanities argument.

nodes.label: Findings that would support the claim
nodes.summary: A described result supports the passage when its outcome is what the author's view predicts.

nodes.label: Findings that would undermine the claim
nodes.summary: A result undermines the passage when its outcome is the opposite of what the author's view would lead one to expect.

nodes.label: Judging the relevance of offered evidence
nodes.summary: Evidence must actually speak to the author's claim; data about a different population or variable may be irrelevant.

nodes.label: Sample representativeness and generalizability
nodes.summary: A finding's force on a general claim depends on whether its sample or setting is representative; an unrepresentative study supports or weakens only weakly.

nodes.label: Argument-Analysis Substrate for RB
nodes.summary: RB reasoning rests on the ability to dissect arguments into claims, evidence, assumptions, and inferences.

nodes.label: Reconstructing argument structure
nodes.summary: Before extending or testing an argument, the reader must separate its conclusion from its supporting premises.

nodes.label: Distinguishing claim from evidence
nodes.summary: The conclusion is what the author wants you to accept; the evidence is what is offered to make you accept it.

nodes.label: The premise-to-conclusion link
nodes.summary: Strengthen/weaken reasoning targets the inferential link between premises and conclusion, not just the truth of either.

nodes.label: Identifying unstated assumptions
nodes.summary: An assumption is an unstated belief the argument needs to be true for its conclusion to follow from its premises.

nodes.label: Necessary versus sufficient assumptions
nodes.summary: A necessary assumption must hold for the argument to work; a sufficient assumption would by itself guarantee the conclusion.

nodes.label: Drawing valid inferences for extension
nodes.summary: RB requires inferences that follow logically from passage-plus-stimulus rather than leaps beyond what is supported.

nodes.label: What counts as a supported inference
nodes.summary: A valid inference must be true if the passage and stimulus are true, requiring no unstated extra leap.

nodes.label: Recognizing an unsupported leap
nodes.summary: A wrong choice may sound reasonable yet require an assumption the passage and stimulus never grant.

nodes.label: Deductive certainty versus probable extension
nodes.summary: RB inferences range from must-be-true deductions to most-likely extrapolations, and the stem signals which level is required.

nodes.label: Causal reasoning in extension
nodes.summary: Many RB stimuli hinge on whether a causal claim survives new information or transfers to a new case.

nodes.label: Correlation versus causation
nodes.summary: A causal conclusion drawn only from correlation is vulnerable to alternative cause, reverse causation, or coincidence.

nodes.label: New information that introduces a confound
nodes.summary: A weakener can supply a third factor that explains both variables, breaking the author's causal claim.

nodes.label: Reverse causation as a weakener
nodes.summary: New information showing the supposed effect actually drives the supposed cause undercuts a causal claim by flipping its direction.

nodes.label: Recognizing reasoning flaws
nodes.summary: Spotting a flaw in the author's reasoning sets up extensions about what would fix or expose it.

nodes.label: Hasty generalization
nodes.summary: Drawing a broad conclusion from too few or unrepresentative cases is a reasoning flaw an RB question may exploit.

nodes.label: False dichotomy
nodes.summary: Treating two options as the only possibilities when others exist is a flaw new information can expose.

nodes.label: Circular reasoning
nodes.summary: An argument whose conclusion is smuggled into its premises offers no real support and cannot be extended soundly.

nodes.label: Ad hominem and straw man
nodes.summary: Attacking the person, or a distorted version of an opposing view, are flaws that can affect how an argument should be applied or judged.

nodes.label: Appeals to authority, emotion, and popularity
nodes.summary: Resting a conclusion on an authority's say-so, an emotional pull, or widespread belief rather than evidence is a flaw RB items can target.

nodes.label: Slippery slope
nodes.summary: Claiming one step inevitably leads to an extreme outcome without justifying each link is a flaw an extension or counterexample can expose.

nodes.label: RB Question Recognition and Strategy
nodes.summary: Recognizing the RB task from the stem and matching it to the right approach is itself a tested skill.

nodes.label: Recognizing RB question stems
nodes.summary: Characteristic phrasings signal which RB move (apply, predict, strengthen, weaken, analogy) the question demands.

nodes.label: Application stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "which of the following is the best example" or "the author would most likely apply this to" signal an application task.

nodes.label: Strengthen/weaken stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "which finding would most undermine" or "would best support the author" signal a new-information task.

nodes.label: Hypothetical and "suppose" stems
nodes.summary: A stem that introduces a new scenario ("suppose that...", "if it were true that...") flags an extrapolation task.

nodes.label: Analogy stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "most analogous to" or "corresponds to the relationship between" flag an analogical-reasoning task.

nodes.label: Prediction / author-response stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "the author would most likely agree that" or "would predict that" flag a task of projecting the author into a new case.

nodes.label: Handling EXCEPT, NOT, and LEAST stems
nodes.summary: Negative stems invert the task, so the credited answer is the odd one out; misreading the negation is a frequent careless error.

nodes.label: A disciplined RB answering process
nodes.summary: A repeatable process keeps the test-taker anchored to the passage while reasoning outward.

nodes.label: Prephrasing the answer
nodes.summary: Forming an expected answer from the passage before reading choices guards against being lured by attractive distractors.

nodes.label: Returning to the relevant passage point
nodes.summary: Even though RB reaches outside the text, the test-taker must re-ground the reasoning in the specific passage idea at issue.

nodes.label: Process of elimination by RB criteria
nodes.summary: Eliminating choices that are out of scope, wrong-direction, or too extreme isolates the choice faithful to the passage.

nodes.label: Choosing the best, not the perfect, answer
nodes.summary: The credited RB answer is the most defensible of the four, even if it is imperfect, so choices are compared against each other.

nodes.label: Suppressing personal opinion and outside facts
nodes.summary: Reasoning beyond the text must still proceed from the author's logic, never from the reader's own views or knowledge.

nodes.label: Parsing exactly what the stem asks
nodes.summary: Before reasoning, the test-taker must pin down the precise RB task and direction (apply vs predict, strengthen vs weaken) the stem sets, since the whole approach follows from it.

nodes.label: Managing time and difficulty on RB items
nodes.summary: Because RB items are dense, pacing and triage decisions affect overall CARS performance.

nodes.label: Triaging hard RB questions
nodes.summary: Flagging and returning to the most abstract RB items protects time for questions that are faster to secure.

nodes.label: Calibrating confidence under uncertainty
nodes.summary: Committing to the best-supported choice and moving on prevents over-investing in a single ambiguous RB item.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Comprehension of the passage's main idea, purpose, and central thesis
Determining authorial purpose, tone, and rhetorical intent
Inferring authorial tone and attitude from word choice and rhetoric
Identifying argument components and distinguishing claim from supporting evidence
Integrating ideas and evaluating the soundness of an argument's assumptions within the text
Drawing inferences that are directly implied by the passage
Integrating distinct passage components into a coherent whole
